elements.sent-2411-001: Spatial transcriptomics (ST) provides essential spatial context by mapping gene expression within tissue, enabling detailed study of cellular heterogeneity and tissue organization.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, aligning ST data with histology images poses challenges due to inherent spatial distortions and modality-specific variations.
elements.sent-2411-003: Existing methods largely rely on direct alignment, which often fails to capture complex cross-modal relationships.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address these limitations, we propose a novel framework that aligns gene and image features using a ranking-based alignment loss, preserving relative similarity across modalities and enabling robust multi-scale alignment.
elements.sent-2411-005: To further enhance the alignment's stability, we employ self-supervised knowledge distillation with a teacher-student network architecture, effectively mitigating disruptions from high dimensionality, sparsity, and noise in gene expression data.
elements.sent-2411-006: Extensive experiments on gene expression prediction and survival analysis demonstrate our framework's effectiveness, showing improved alignment and predictive performance over existing methods and establishing a robust tool for gene-guided image representation learning in digital pathology.
elements.sent-2411-007: Digital pathology has advanced significantly in recent years, thanks to the availability of large number of digitized slides and rapid development of deep learning.
elements.sent-2411-008: Powerful learning methods have been proposed for image-based prediction at different scales, including individual cells \cite{graham2019hover, abousamra2021multi, horst2024cellvit , larger structures like glands \cite{isensee2021nnu, wang2022uctransnet, xu2025semi , regions of interest \cite{le2020utilizing, dominguez2024systematic , and the whole slide \cite{ilse2018attention,li2021dual,lu2021data,kapse2024si,chen2021multimodal .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: However, these methods, despite their promise, are fundamentally limited by the amount of information one can extract from a digital slide.
elements.sent-2411-010: Most clinical outcomes are given at a whole slide-level.
elements.sent-2411-011: Due to the heterogeneity of the tissue microenvironment, it is very difficult for a machine learning algorithm to directly connect localized cell morphology and spatial context with clinical outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-012: Connecting cell morphology/context with their actual functionality is the missing link and can be the key to advancing image-based diagnosis and prognosis.
elements.sent-2411-013: Transcriptomics \cite{mortazavi2008mapping, wang2009rna reflects the genome-wide quantification of gene expression within a tissue, and these approaches provide a snapshot of gene activity within a variably heterogeneous collection of cells.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Single-cell RNA sequencing \cite{tang2009mrna, svensson2018exponential attempts to address this cellular heterogeneity by profiling gene expression within individual level, enabling more precise cell typing and characterization.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Despite its high resolution, this approach disrupts tissue architecture and loses the critical spatial context that defines an organized tissue \cite{saliba2014single .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Spatial transcriptomics (ST) bridges the gap by enabling gene expression profiling within tissue structures, preserving the spatial context of cellular activity \cite{rao2021exploring .}
elements.sent-2411-017: It measures gene expression at specific locations, allowing for the mapping of cellular states and molecular interactions in their native micro-environments \cite{staahl2016visualization, marx2021method .}
elements.sent-2411-018: This spatial dimension is crucial for studying the interactions between cells and their microenvironment, which are central to understanding disease dynamics and therapeutic responses \cite{moffitt2018molecular, ding2022image .}
elements.sent-2411-019: For example, ST can uncover molecular signatures unique to specific tumor regions, such as the core, invasive margins, and immune-infiltrated zones, enabling a nuanced view of tumor heterogeneity \cite{ji2020multimodal .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1.0\textwidth]{figures-arxiv/tsne.png} \caption{t-SNE visualization \cite{van2008visualizing} of image representation from different methods. (a) Natural-Image-SSL. (b) Histopath-Image-SSL. (c) Gene-Image CL (d) Gene-Image CL with ranking loss. (e) RankByGene. We first use K-means clustering to assign a class label to each spot based on gene expression values. Then, we apply t-SNE to visualize the image features corresponding to each spot and color them according to their assigned class labels. A higher v-score \cite{rosenberg2007v} indicates that the image representation more precisely captures the distribution of gene expression values. Adding ranking loss (d) enhances the separability between image features of different classes. Our method (e), with both ranking loss and distillation shows the highest separability.} \label{fig:tsne}
elements.sent-2411-020: ST data provides new opportunities to enhance existing image-based prediction methods with novel biological information.
elements.sent-2411-021: Most ST data have both localized gene expressions and their corresponding histology image patches.
elements.sent-2411-022: One can exploit the two complementary modalities at a fine scale, unveiling the subtle visual cues from cell morphology and context that capture cell functionality.
elements.sent-2411-023: In this paper, we study the problem of learning gene-guided image representations through ST data.
elements.sent-2411-024: In particular, we use the gene expression to better align image features, so that these image features encode cell-functionality-specific visual information.
elements.sent-2411-025: As we will demonstrate in experiments, these gene-guided image features will improve image-based prediction of clinical outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-026: The learned features can be applied broadly to any image-only cohort, benefiting the digital pathology community.
elements.sent-2411-027: Despite its scientific potential, multi-modal representation learning with ST data is challenging for various reasons.
elements.sent-2411-028: First and most importantly, it is fundamentally unknown how well the image and gene expressions could align.
elements.sent-2411-029: Different modalities may contain different information; ensuring complete alignment can be difficult or even impossible \cite{huh2024position .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Secondly, the image and gene features are encoded with different networks and have different initial representation powers; while images can be encoded with CNNs and can be initialized with powerful foundational models \cite{wang2021transpath,chen2022scaling,chen2024uni,xu2024whole,wang2024pathology,vorontsov2024foundation , typically gene feature encoding can only be based on fully-connected networks and trained from scratch \cite{xie2024spatially,jaume2024hest,min2024multimodal .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Furthermore, the gene expression data is extremely high-dimensional (with more than 15,000 genes per sample) and very sparse (on average, 80\% of gene expression values are zeros \cite{jaume2024hest ).}
elements.sent-2411-032: Finally, as a novel technology, available ST data often suffers from experimental artifacts, such as spatial dependent noise \cite{abrar2023novatest and sparsity \cite{li2022sd2,li2023comprehensive .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: To address these challenges, methods have been proposed to map both image patches and gene expressions into a shared latent feature space.
elements.sent-2411-034: Self-supervised contrastive learning losses have been adopted to ensure the image features and gene features from the same location (called a spot) are mapped together~\cite{jaume2024hest .}
elements.sent-2411-035: The standard contrastive loss, however, overlooks the potential similarity between different spots.
elements.sent-2411-036: Xie et al.~\cite{xie2024spatially employ a smoothed variant of the CLIP loss~\cite{radford2021learning to encourage spots with similar image patches or similar gene expressions to be mapped closer in the feature space.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: These methods, however, do not fully address the aforementioned challenges and lack an effective global strategy to align multi-modal features.
elements.sent-2411-038: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to align image and gene features across multiple scales, both locally and globally.
elements.sent-2411-039: The alignment method is designed to be robust to modality-specific distortions, addressing the challenges outlined earlier.
elements.sent-2411-040: Specifically, we introduce an innovative ranking-based alignment loss that captures the relative similarity ordering of feature representations across modalities.
elements.sent-2411-041: By enforcing consistency in cross-modal ranking, we ensure that the similarity relationships between gene expression features are reflected in the corresponding image features.
elements.sent-2411-042: This ranking-based approach offers several key advantages.
elements.sent-2411-043: First, it facilitates alignment across both local and global scales, enabling even distant features to interact and align.
elements.sent-2411-044: This global alignment complements the existing local matching of image and gene features of the same spot~\cite{xie2024spatially, min2024multimodal .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Second, by focusing on matching rankings rather than exact similarity values, our method is more robust to distortions, allowing it to tolerate similarity variations especially between features that are far apart from one another.
elements.sent-2411-046: To further enhance the robustness of the alignment process, we adopt a self-supervised knowledge distillation approach to stabilize the learning of gene-informed image representations.
elements.sent-2411-047: By utilizing a teacher-student network architecture with both weakly and strongly augmented image patches, we ensure that the student network effectively learns the aligned features.
elements.sent-2411-048: This approach helps the student network remain resilient to potential disruptions arising from imperfections in the gene expressions, such as high dimensionality, sparsity, and noisy or missing values.
elements.sent-2411-049: Compared to other methods, the image features generated by our approach effectively capture the distribution of gene expression values.
elements.sent-2411-050: In summary, we propose a novel multi-modal feature alignment method that integrates pathology images and ST data, enabling robust gene-informed image representation learning.
elements.sent-2411-052: We introduce a new cross-modal ranking consistency mechanism to enhance multi-modal alignment, achieving alignment at both local and global scales while remaining robust to distortions over long-range distances.
elements.sent-2411-053: We apply a knowledge distillation approach to the multi-modal alignment task, further improving the robustness of the alignment process despite imperfections in gene expression features.
elements.sent-2411-054: Extensive experiments across multiple downstream tasks, including gene expression prediction and survival prediction, demonstrate the superior performance of our framework compared to existing self-supervised alignment methods.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures-arxiv/framework.png} \caption{Overview of our RankbyGene framework. The framework begins with WSI Tiling, where WSIs are divided into tiles, each paired with a gene profile. In the feature extraction part, weak and strong augmentations of the image tiles are processed through a teacher encoder and student encoder, while a gene encoder extracts features from the gene profile. The feature alignment stage ensures that weakly and strongly augmented image features are aligned through intra-modal distillation loss and the image and gene features are aligned using gene-image contrastive loss. Meanwhile, our proposed cross-modal ranking consistency loss maintains consistent similarity ranking orders across two modalities. } \label{fig:overview}
elements.sent-2411-055: Histopathology image analysis is regarded as the gold standard for cancer diagnosis and treatment \cite{lu2021ai,niazi2019digital,barisoni2020digital .}
elements.sent-2411-056: Due to the huge resolution of whole slide images (WSIs) and the difficulty of obtaining patch-level labels, WSI analysis is commonly performed in a weakly supervised setting \cite{dietterich1997solving , where only slide-level labels are available.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Typical WSI analysis tasks include classification \cite{ilse2018attention,li2021dual,zhang2022dtfd,shao2021transmil,qu2023boosting,tang2023multiple,zhang2023attention , which predicts the presence or absence of a tumor or identifies specific tumor subtypes, and survival prediction \cite{chen2021multimodal,xu2023multimodal,zhou2023cross,zhang2024prototypical,song2024multimodal , which estimates patient mortality risk.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Many multiple instance learning (MIL) \cite{ilse2018attention,li2021dual,zhang2022dtfd,qu2022bi,lin2023interventional,zhu2025dgr based frameworks have been proposed to model relationships between patches more effectively, enabling more accurate slide-level predictions.}
elements.sent-2411-059: The performance of WSI analysis heavily relies on image feature learning.
elements.sent-2411-060: With advancements in self-supervised learning (SSL) in computer vision, methods like SimCLR \cite{chen2020simple , MoCo \cite{he2020momentum , and DINO \cite{caron2021emerging have been adapted for image feature learning in histopathology, leading to the development of various foundation models pretrained on WSI, such as CTransPath \cite{wang2021transpath , HIPT \cite{chen2022scaling , and more recent models like UNI \cite{chen2024uni , GigaPath \cite{xu2024whole , CHIEF \cite{wang2024pathology , and Virchow \cite{vorontsov2024foundation .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: These foundation models have significantly improved WSI analysis performance.
elements.sent-2411-062: Additionally, task-specific fine-tuning \cite{liu2023multiple,li2023task,tang2024feature techniques have been introduced to further optimize image feature learning for downstream tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-063: With the growing availability of multi-modal data and advancements in pretraining techniques across multiple modalities, CLIP-based \cite{radford2021learning,andonian2022robust,goel2022cyclip,li2023scaling,zhang2024rankclip vision-language pretraining methods have been applied to text-image pathology datasets, resulting in the development of approaches like MI-zero \cite{lu2023visual , PLIP \cite{huang2023visual , and Conch \cite{lu2024avisionlanguage .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Furthermore, the recent availability of ST data \cite{jaume2024hest, chen2024stimage has expanded opportunities for multimodal learning in histopathology.}
elements.sent-2411-065: More challenging tasks, such as gene expression prediction \cite{jaume2024hest,chung2024accurate,chen2024stimage , have been introduced to evaluate the robustness of pretrained image encoders.}
elements.sent-2411-066: In addition, several methods for image-gene pretraining, such as BLEEP \cite{xie2024spatially and mclSTExp \cite{min2024multimodal , have also been proposed to enhance image feature learning.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: However, these approaches may not fully capture relationships between spots, nor adequately address the noise and sparsity in gene expression data.
elements.sent-2411-068: In this work, we focus on developing a robust multi-modal alignment approach that integrates image and gene features across multiple scales to achieve more effective gene-informed image representations.
elements.sent-2411-069: Our learning pipeline is illustrated in \Cref{fig:overview .}
elements.sent-2411-070: The training data consists of pairs of image patches and gene expression profiles, with each pair corresponding to a specific tissue spot.
elements.sent-2411-071: Image patches are fed into image encoders, which include both a teacher and a student encoder to facilitate knowledge distillation for gene-guided image feature learning.
elements.sent-2411-072: This setup helps stabilize the image feature learning process during alignment with gene features.
elements.sent-2411-073: Meanwhile, gene expression data is processed by a gene encoder.
elements.sent-2411-074: Both image and gene features are mapped into a shared latent feature space.
elements.sent-2411-075: To align these features, we employ a contrastive learning loss that ensures the image and gene features of the same tissue spot are closely matched.
elements.sent-2411-076: Additionally, we introduce a cross-modal ranking consistency loss to maintain the same relative ranking of similarity across spots, ensuring that for any given spot, its similarity to other spots is consistent in both image and gene feature spaces.
elements.sent-2411-077: At inference time, we will only use the teacher image encoder to extract gene-guided image features from histopathology image patches.
elements.sent-2411-078: The feature can be used for different downstream tasks that make predictions based on histopathology images.
elements.sent-2411-079: We will first introduce the contrastive learning loss we use for spot-wise alignment, gene-image contrastive (\Cref{sec:infonce ).}
elements.sent-2411-080: Next, we introduce the cross-modal ranking consistency loss (\Cref{sec:ranking ).}
elements.sent-2411-081: We conclude this section with details on the intra-modal distillation for gene-guided image features (\Cref{sec:distillation ).. \myparagraph{Preliminaries.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Given a set of $N$ spots, $\{1,\ldots,N\}$, each with a pair of image patch and gene profile, we send them into two separate encoders (image encoder and gene encoder), mapping them into image features and gene features.
elements.sent-2411-083: To align image and gene features, we first employ the InfoNCE Loss~\cite{oord2018representation , a commonly used loss function for aligning two modalities.}
elements.sent-2411-084: InfoNCE encourages the model to pull positive pairs (image and gene features from the same spot) closer in the shared latent space while pushing apart negative pairs (image and gene features from different spots).
elements.sent-2411-085: The Gene-Image Contrastive loss is defined as follows: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\text{gene-image}} = -\sum_{p=1}^N \left[\log \frac{\exp(\text{sim}(i_p, g_p) / \tau)}{\sum_{q=1}^N \exp(\text{sim}(i_p, g_q) / \tau)}\right] \label{eq:infonce}\end{equation} where $\text{sim (i_\ast, g_\ast)$ is the cosine similarity between the image feature $i_\ast$ and the gene feature $g_\ast$.}
elements.sent-2411-086: This loss penalizes unmatched pairs by reducing their similarity while increasing the similarity between matched gene-image pairs.
elements.sent-2411-087: The InfoNCE loss ensures local alignment between image and gene features from the same tissue spot, but it does not address global alignment, which is essential for achieving more accurate and consistent cross-modal correspondences.
elements.sent-2411-088: Directly aligning distances between features from distant tissue spots is not practical, as long-range feature relationships may not be reliable.
elements.sent-2411-089: Instead, we propose that the relative ranking of distances between features is more robust and can provide a more trustworthy basis for alignment.
elements.sent-2411-090: To leverage this idea, we introduce the Cross-Modal Ranking Consistency Loss.
elements.sent-2411-091: This loss function encourages the model to learn image representations while maintaining the relative similarity ordering of gene features across tissue spots.
elements.sent-2411-092: By focusing on the ranking of distances rather than exact alignments, the ranking loss facilitates a more reliable and robust global alignment.
elements.sent-2411-093: It complements the local alignment achieved by InfoNCE, while also capturing long-range interactions between features from different tissue spots.
elements.sent-2411-094: In doing so, the ranking consistency loss promotes a more comprehensive and stable cross-modal alignment, improving both local and global correspondences.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures-arxiv/rankbygene.pdf} \caption{Illustration of the ranking loss intuition, when the gene features $g_q$ is closer to $g_p$ than $g_r$. Note the similarity is inverse proportional to the distance. Left: $i_q$ is also closer to $i_p$ than $i_r$, and furthermore, and the gap between image feature similarities $S_{p,q}^I$ and $S_{p,r}^I$ is bigger than the gap between gene feature similarities $S_{p,q}^I$ and $S_{p,r}^I$. This is the desirable case where $\ell(p,q,r)$ is negative. Middle: when the similarity ranking is the same between gene and image features, but when the gap between image feature similarities is smaller than the gap between gene feature similarities. $\ell(p,q,r)$ is positive, and will incur penalty. Right: when the similarity order is inconsistent, $\ell(p,q,r)$ is positive. Undesirable.} \label{fig:ranking}
elements.sent-2411-095: Our ranking loss is illustrated in~\Cref{fig:ranking .}
elements.sent-2411-096: For a spot of interest, $p$, we want to ensure the similarity between $p$ and two other spots, $q$ and $r$, are ranked consistently in both image feature and gene feature.
elements.sent-2411-097: For simplicity, we denote by $S^I_{p,q =\text{sim (i_p,i_q)$ and $S^G_{p,q =\text{sim (g_p,g_q)$ the similarity between $p$ and $q$'s image features and gene features.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: We also denote by $S^I_{p,r}$ and $S^G_{p,r}$ the image and gene feature similarity between $p$ and $r$.
elements.sent-2411-100: In other words, $g_q$ is closer to $g_p$ compared with $g_r$.
elements.sent-2411-101: We would like to ensure the image features $i_q$ and $i_r$ also maintain the same order.
elements.sent-2411-102: Classic ordinal ranking loss \cite{burges2005learning will just require the image feature similarity difference $S^I_{p,q - S^I_{p,r}$ to be bigger than a fixed positive value $\epsilon$.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: However, this may be undesirable especially for long interactions.
elements.sent-2411-104: To better preserve the relative positions between features, we require the difference $S^I_{p,q - S^I_{p,r}$ to be bigger than $S^G_{p,q - S^G_{p,r}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: Formally, we define the function \begin{equation}\ell(p, q, r) = \left(S^G_{p, q} - S^G_{p, r}\right) - \left(S^I_{p, q} - S^I_{p, r}\right) \label{eq:similarity_difference}\end{equation} and intend to enforce $\ell(p,q,r)$ to be non-positive for all triplets $(p,q,r)$.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\ell(p, q, r) = \left(S^G_{p, q} - S^G_{p, r}\right) - \left(S^I_{p, q} - S^I_{p, r}\right) \label{eq:similarity_difference}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-106: In practice, we rewrite the function with the sign function \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \ell(p, q, r) = \text{sign} (S^G_{p, q} - S^G_{p, r}) \cdot \left[\left(S^G_{p, q} - S^G_{p, r}\right) \right. \left. - \left(S^I_{p, q} - S^I_{p, r}\right)\right] \end{aligned} \label{eq:sign_similarity_difference}\end{equation} This way, even if $S^G_{p,q < S^G_{p,r}$, requiring $\ell(\cdot)$ to be non-positive will still enforce the relative ranking consistency between image and gene features of the three spots.}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation} \ell(p, q, r) = sign (S^G_{p, q} - S^G_{p, r}) \cdot \left[\left(S^G_{p, q} - S^G_{p, r}\right) \right. \left. - \left(S^I_{p, q} - S^I_{p, r}\right)\right]  \label{eq:sign_similarity_difference}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-107: Finally, we need a loss to enforce $\ell(\cdot)$ to be nonnegative.
elements.sent-2411-108: To this end, we use a variant of the classic hinge loss, $\max\left\{0,\ell(p,q,r)\right\}$, which incurs penalty if and only if $\ell(p,q,r)$ is positive.
elements.sent-2411-109: Enumerating through all triplets, we have the cross-modal ranking consistency loss \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\text{rank}} = \sum_{p}\sum_{q\neq p}\sum_{r\neq p,q} \max\left\{0, \ell(p,q,r)\right\} \label{eq:ranklosse_e}\end{equation} \myparagraph{Accelerating the Computation.}
elements.sent-2411-110: During training, at each iteration, we will apply the loss to all spots within a mini-batch.
elements.sent-2411-111: However, this can still be computationally expensive, as it compares $O(N^3)$ pairs of spots.
elements.sent-2411-112: To alleviate the burden, we still enumerate through all $p$'s.
elements.sent-2411-113: But for each $p$, instead of enumerating through all $O(N^2)$ $(q,r)$ pairs, we only sample $O(N)$ random $(q,r)$ pairs.
elements.sent-2411-114: In particular, taking the list of spots except $p$, $L=(1,\ldots,p-1,p+1,\ldots,N)$.
elements.sent-2411-115: We randomly shuffle the sequence, getting a shuffled list, $L'$.
elements.sent-2411-116: We go through the list, $L'$, each time taking two consecutive spots as $q$ and $r$.
elements.sent-2411-117: For the last spot, we will pair it with the first spot in $L'$.
elements.sent-2411-118: This gives us $N-1$ sample $(q,r)$ pairs, and ensures each spot appears in two of the sampled pairs.
elements.sent-2411-119: This reasonably covers similarity rankings of all the spots, but only using $O(N^2)$ triplets.
elements.sent-2411-120: In our framework, we employ a teacher-student network architecture to achieve robust feature representations across differently augmented instances of the same pathology image, drawing on recent advances in self-supervised knowledge distillation for single-modality representation learning \cite{caron2021emerging, oquab2023dinov2 .}
elements.sent-2411-121: To enhance stability and invariance in feature embeddings produced by the patch encoder, we apply both weak and strong augmentations to simulate the typical variability found in pathology images.
elements.sent-2411-122: In this setup, the weakly augmented image is processed through the teacher encoder, while the strongly augmented version passes through the student encoder.
elements.sent-2411-123: The weights of the teacher encoder are incrementally updated using an Exponential Moving Average (EMA) of the student encoder's weights, which helps stabilize the training.
elements.sent-2411-124: This strategy ensures that the student gradually learns stable features over time.
elements.sent-2411-125: To enforce the consistency between the representations of the two augmented versions, we introduce the Image Consistency Loss: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\text{distil}} = - \frac{1}{N} \sum_{p=1}^N \left[\log \frac{\exp(\text{sim}(i_{p}^w, i_{p}^s) / \tau)}{\sum_{q=1}^N \exp(\text{sim}(i_{p}^w, i_{q}^s) / \tau)} \right]\end{equation} where $N$ is the batch size, $i_{p ^w$ is the feature obtained from the weakly augmented image using the teacher image encoder, and $i_{p ^s$ is the feature from the strongly augmented image using the student image encoder.}}
elements.sent-2411-126: Minimizing this loss encourages the image encoder to learn representations resilient to such variations and potential disruptions from gene expression data characteristics, such as high dimensionality, sparsity, noise, and missing values.
elements.sent-2411-127: The total loss is a weighted sum of the Gene-Image Contrastive Loss, the Cross-Modal Ranking Loss, and the Intra-Modal Distillation Loss: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{gene-image}} + \lambda_1 \mathcal{L}_{\text{rank}} + \lambda_2 \mathcal{L}_{\text{distil}} \label{eq:totalloss}\end{equation} where \( \lambda_1 \) and \( \lambda_2 \) are hyperparameters that control the balance between the ranking consistency loss and the distillation loss, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-128: In this study, we use the breast and lung ST data from the HEST-1k dataset \cite{jaume2024hest , which collects publicly available, high-quality ST datasets and applies standardized processing to all collected data.}

elements.sent-2411-175: We define Rank Accuracy as a metric to evaluate whether the ranking relationships among gene features are effectively captured by the image features during training.
elements.sent-2411-176: After the end of each epoch, Rank Accuracy is calculated as follows: the first gene-image pair in each batch is randomly selected as the anchor pair, along with two additional target pairs.
elements.sent-2411-177: Rank Accuracy is determined by checking whether the target pair with higher gene feature similarity to the anchor pair also has a higher image feature similarity.
elements.sent-2411-178: This process is repeated eight times to calculate the final Rank Accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-179: In \Cref{fig:rankacc , we compare the change of the rank accuracy during training between different methods.}
elements.sent-2411-180: The figure shows that our method achieves a larger improvement in accuracy during training compared to the baseline methods, suggesting that the rank relationships in the gene features are effectively captured by image features during the training process.

elements.sent-2411-181: To evaluate the accuracy of our model’s predictions for critical genes, we selected PTGES3 as the marker gene for visualization on the Breast-ST1 dataset due to its association with poor prognosis in breast invasive carcinoma across two independent cohorts \cite{protein .}

elements.sent-2411-185: Our proposed framework establishes a robust and scalable approach for efficiently aligning spatial transcriptomics data with histopathology images, overcoming key challenges in cross-modal alignment.
elements.sent-2411-186: We propose a ranking-based mechanism to capture similarity relationships between gene and image features, facilitating alignment that is resilient to distortions and effective across both local and global scales.
elements.sent-2411-187: This approach preserves essential spatial and molecular patterns, which is critical for accurately capturing the biological complexity of tissue structures.
elements.sent-2411-188: Additionally, our use of a self-supervised knowledge distillation method further enhances the alignment process, enabling the model to adaptively handle the high dimensionality, sparsity, and noise inherent in gene expression data.
elements.sent-2411-189: Through comprehensive experiments, we demonstrate our framework not only improves alignment quality but also enhances predictive capabilities in gene expression prediction and survival analysis.
elements.sent-2411-190: Ultimately, this work provides a foundational tool for multi-modal representation learning in digital pathology.

elements.sent-2411-231: In this study, we assess the performance of gene expression prediction on a per-sample basis.
elements.sent-2411-232: Specifically, we evaluate each model using three metrics: Pearson Correlation Coefficient (PCC), Mean Squared Error (MSE), and Mean Absolute Error (MAE).
elements.sent-2411-233: These metrics are computed for each gene at each spot and then aggregated to provide an overall evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-234: Assume that an ST sample contains $n$ spots, each with $m$ genes.
elements.sent-2411-235: For the $j$-th gene in the $i$-th spot, the PCC ($PCC_{i,j}$) is calculated as: \begin{equation}PCC_{i,j} = \frac{(\hat{y}_{i,j} - \bar{\hat{y}}_{i,j})(y_{i,j} - \bar{y}_{i,j})}{\sqrt{(\hat{y}_{i,j} - \bar{\hat{y}}_{i,j})^2} \sqrt{(y_{i,j} - \bar{y}_{i,j})^2}} \label{eq:pcc_ij}\end{equation} where $ \hat{y _{i,j $ and $ y_{i,j $ are the predicted and ground truth expression values of the $j$-th gene at the $i$-th spot, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-236: To aggregate the PCC across all $n$ spots for a specific gene $j$, we calculate $PCC_j$ as follows: \begin{equation}PCC_j = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (\hat{y}_{i,j} - \bar{\hat{y}}_{.,j})(y_{i,j} - \bar{y}_{.,j})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (\hat{y}_{i,j} - \bar{\hat{y}}_{.,j})^2} \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_{i,j} - \bar{y}_{.,j})^2}} \label{eq:pcc_j}\end{equation} Finally, we take the average of $PCC_j$ to compute the overall PCC across $m$ genes: \begin{equation}PCC = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} PCC_j \label{eq:pcc_avg}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-237: MAE measures the average absolute difference between the predicted and ground truth values and is calculated as: \begin{equation}MAE(Y, \hat{Y}) = \frac{1}{n \cdot m} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left|y_{i,j} - \hat{y}_{i,j}\right| \label{eq:mae}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-238: MSE quantifies the average squared difference between the predicted and ground truth values, defined as: \begin{equation}MSE(Y, \hat{Y}) = \frac{1}{n \cdot m} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} (y_{i,j} - \hat{y}_{i,j})^2 \label{eq:mse}\end{equation} Since we perform gene-image alignment using a selected list of cancer-related genes, we only evaluate the genes included in this list.
elements.sent-2411-239: When there are multiple ST samples in the test set, we calculate PCC, MAE, and MSE for each sample using the above formulas.
elements.sent-2411-240: The final results are then obtained by averaging these metrics across all samples.
elements.sent-2411-241: Following previous methods \cite{chen2021multimodal,zhou2023cross,xu2023multimodal , we perform 5-fold cross-validation to evaluate the model on the survival analysis task, reporting the cross-validated concordance index (C-Index) and its standard deviation (std) as metrics.}

elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=.9\textwidth]{figures/frameworkv8.png} \caption{Overview of our RankbyGene framework. The framework begins with WSI Tiling, where WSIs are cut into patches, each paired with a gene spot. In feature extraction, weak and strong augmentations of the patches are processed through a teacher and student encoder, while a gene encoder extracts features from the gene profile. elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width=.75\textwidth]{figures/rankbygene_fig3.pdf} \caption{Illustration of the ranking loss intuition, when the gene features $g_q$ is closer to $g_p$ than $g_r$. Note the similarity is inverse proportional to the distance. Left: $i_q$ is also closer to $i_p$ than $i_r$, and furthermore, and the gap between image feature similarities $S_{p,q}^I$ and $S_{p,r}^I$ is bigger than the gap between gene feature similarities $S_{p,q}^I$ and $S_{p,r}^I$. This is the desirable case where $\ell(p,q,r)$ is negative. Middle: when the similarity ranking is the same between gene and image features, but when the gap between image feature similarities is smaller than the gap between gene feature similarities. $\ell(p,q,r)$ is positive, and will incur penalty. Right: when the similarity order is inconsistent, $\ell(p,q,r)$ is positive. Undesirable.} \label{fig:ranking}
elements.sent-2411-288: Our ranking loss is inspired by the classic ordinal ranking loss \cite{burges2005learning , but is modified to better address challenges in our gene-image alignment task.}
elements.sent-2411-289: As illustrated in~\Cref{fig:ranking , for a spot of interest, $p$, we want to ensure the similarity between $p$ and two other spots, $q$ and $r$, are ranked consistently in both image feature and gene feature.}

elements.sent-2411-291: To further accelerate the alignment, we propose a modified ranking loss that pushes image features further apart if possible.
elements.sent-2411-292: In our new ranking loss, we forcing similarity among images to be bigger than similarity among corresponding genes; it pushes image features apart while preserving the ranking.
elements.sent-2411-293: Formally, we require the difference $S^I_{p,q - S^I_{p,r}$ to be bigger than $S^G_{p,q - S^G_{p,r}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-294: We define \begin{equation}\ell(p, q, r) = \left(S^G_{p, q} - S^G_{p, r}\right) - \left(S^I_{p, q} - S^I_{p, r}\right) \label{eq:similarity_difference}\end{equation} and intend to enforce $\ell(p,q,r)$ to be non-positive for all triplets $(p,q,r)$.

elements.sent-2411-348: We present a robust and scalable approach for aligning spatial transcriptomics data with histopathology images, addressing major obstacles in cross-modal alignment.
elements.sent-2411-349: This method employs a ranking-based mechanism to capture similarity relationships between gene and image features, ensuring resilience to distortions and effectiveness across local and global scales while preserving crucial spatial and molecular patterns.
elements.sent-2411-350: A self-supervised knowledge distillation procedure further refines alignment by handling high dimensionality, sparsity, and noise in gene expression data.
elements.sent-2411-351: Through extensive experiments, our framework not only improves alignment quality, but also enhances predictive performance in gene expression prediction, slide-level classification, and survival analysis.
elements.sent-2411-352: This work thus provides a foundational tool for advancing multi-modal representation learning in digital pathology.

elements.sent-2411-396: Please follow the steps and style guidelines outlined below for submitting your author response.
elements.sent-2411-397: The author rebuttal is optional and, following similar guidelines to previous conferences, is meant to provide you with an opportunity to rebut factual errors or to supply additional information requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-398: It is NOT intended to add new contributions (theorems, algorithms, experiments) that were absent in the original submission and NOT specifically requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-399: You may optionally add a figure, graph, or proof to your rebuttal to better illustrate your answer to the reviewers' comments.
elements.sent-2411-400: Per a passed 2018 PAMI-TC motion, reviewers should refrain from requesting significant additional experiments for the rebuttal or penalize for lack of additional experiments.
elements.sent-2411-401: Authors should refrain from including new experimental results in the rebuttal, especially when not specifically requested to do so by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-402: Authors may include figures with illustrations or comparison tables of results reported in the submission/supplemental material or in other papers.
